facts.text: Cayuga Heights was incorporated as a village in 1915, covering half a square mile north of Ithaca.
The village’s streets were designed by landscape architect Harold Caparn, who also created Brooklyn Botanic Garden, giving Cayuga Heights a curving, park-like layout.
In 1952, Cayuga Heights opened a wastewater treatment plant on the shore of Cayuga Lake, one of the first in the region.
In 2012, the village board abandoned a plan to cull deer and instead chose to capture and sterilize does, reflecting a shift in wildlife policy.
Cayuga Heights sits directly north of Cornell University’s main campus and hosts many faculty, including the university’s president.
The village’s name reflects its position overlooking Cayuga Lake, a reminder of the early military tract given to veterans after the Revolutionary War.
